news_summary: Recent health advancements offer promising breakthroughs in vision protection, innovative cancer treatments, healthier cooking methods, and unique research insights from unconventional experiences.
news_summary_fr: Les récentes avancées dans le domaine de la santé offrent des percées prometteuses en matière de protection de la vue, de traitements innovants contre le cancer, de méthodes de cuisson plus saines, ainsi que des perspectives de recherche uniques issues d'expériences non conventionnelles.
news_summary_es: Los recientes avances en el ámbito de la salud ofrecen avances prometedores en la protección de la vista, tratamientos innovadores contra el cáncer, métodos de cocina más saludables y conocimientos únicos derivados de experiencias poco convencionales.

articles.title: Breakthrough could protect the vision cells that let you see faces and colors
articles.summary: Scientists have taken a major step toward protecting the very cells that make sharp, colorful vision possible. By testing more than 2,700 compounds in thousands of lab-grown human retinal models, researchers uncovered several molecules that can shield cone photoreceptors—the cells responsible for reading, recognizing faces, and seeing color—from degeneration. They also identified a key protective mechanism involving casein kinase 1, offering a promising new target for treatment.
articles.content:
A research team led by Botond Roska at the Institute of Molecular and Clinical Ophthalmology Basel (IOB), together with collaborators, has uncovered genetic pathways and compounds that can help protect cone photoreceptors. These cells are damaged in diseases such as age-related macular degeneration, a leading cause of vision loss.

Cone photoreceptors are concentrated in the macula and are essential for reading, recognizing faces, and seeing color. When these cells die, as they do in many inherited retinal disorders and macular degeneration, central vision is lost. Despite decades of scientific effort, no approved treatments can stop this process. This study, led by first authors Stefan Spirig, Alvaro Herrero Navarro and colleagues, aims to address this gap using a human-based experimental system.

Large-Scale Screening of Compounds in Human Retinal Models

To explore potential treatments, researchers tested more than 2,700 compounds across 20,000 human retinal organoids. The results revealed both promising leads and important safety concerns:

Some classes of compounds damaged cone cells, highlighting potential risks

Several molecules were found to protect cone photoreceptors from degeneration

Blocking casein kinase 1 emerged as a key protective strategy

Scientists selectively labeled cone photoreceptors so they could track how these cells responded over time under controlled stress conditions that mimic disease. This setup made it possible to systematically evaluate compounds with known molecular targets.

Key Protective Mechanism Confirmed

The analysis revealed consistent patterns. Two kinase inhibitors repeatedly showed strong protective effects, helping cone cells survive for extended periods. These benefits were observed across different stress conditions and were also confirmed in a mouse model of retinal degeneration, suggesting the findings may apply more broadly.

In addition to identifying protective pathways, the team has released a detailed dataset covering all tested compounds, their molecular targets, and their effects on cone survival in human tissue. This resource is expected to support the development of therapies aimed at preserving central vision and help researchers assess potential retinal toxicity more systematically.

Moving Closer to Preventing Vision Loss

By combining retinal biology, organoid technology, and large-scale compound screening, this research provides a strong foundation for future treatments. It advances a long-standing goal in ophthalmology: protecting the cells that make vision possible.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough in protecting cone photoreceptors, which are crucial for sharp, colorful vision and are damaged in common retinal diseases causing vision loss. The research uses large-scale screening of compounds in human retinal models, identifies protective molecules and mechanisms, and offers a promising new target for treatment. This has broad implications for preventing vision loss in millions, addressing a major unmet medical need with substantial positive real-world impact.
articles.personality_title: Scientists discover new way to protect cells key to clear, colorful vision
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Cone photoreceptors are special cells in the eye that help us see colors, read, and recognize faces. These cells are found in the macula, the center part of the retina. When they get damaged, as in diseases like age-related macular degeneration, people lose their central vision. Until now, there have been no treatments to stop this damage.

**What happened** – A team led by Botond Roska at the Institute of Molecular and Clinical Ophthalmology Basel tested over 2,700 chemical compounds on 20,000 lab-grown human retinal models. They looked for substances that could protect cone cells from dying. They found several molecules that helped keep these cells healthy. They also discovered that blocking an enzyme called casein kinase 1 can protect the cones.

**Impact** – This finding is important because it points to a new way to stop vision loss caused by cone cell damage. The study used human tissue models and confirmed results in mice, making the discovery more reliable. The team also shared detailed data about how each compound affects cone cells, which can help other scientists develop safe and effective treatments.

**What's next step** – Researchers will now focus on turning these protective molecules into real medicines. They will test them further to make sure they work well and are safe for people. This work could lead to new treatments that save the sight of millions affected by retinal diseases.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists have identified chemicals and a key enzyme that protect the eye’s color and detail-sensing cells, opening the door to future treatments for vision loss.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques découvrent une nouvelle façon de protéger les cellules clés de la vision claire et colorée
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les photorécepteurs en cônes sont des cellules spéciales de l'œil qui nous aident à voir les couleurs, lire et reconnaître les visages. Ces cellules se trouvent dans la macula, la partie centrale de la rétine. Quand elles sont endommagées, comme dans des maladies telles que la dégénérescence maculaire liée à l’âge, les personnes perdent leur vision centrale. Jusqu’à présent, aucun traitement ne pouvait arrêter ces dommages.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une équipe dirigée par Botond Roska à l’Institut d’Ophtalmologie Moléculaire et Clinique de Bâle a testé plus de 2 700 composés chimiques sur 20 000 modèles rétiniens humains cultivés en laboratoire. Ils ont cherché des molécules capables de protéger les cellules en cônes contre la mort. Plusieurs ont aidé à garder ces cellules en bonne santé. Ils ont aussi découvert que bloquer une enzyme appelée kinase 1 de la caséine protège les cônes.

**Impact** – Cette découverte est importante car elle montre une nouvelle façon d’arrêter la perte de vision causée par les dommages aux cellules en cônes. L’étude a utilisé des modèles humains et confirmé les résultats chez la souris, ce qui rend la découverte plus fiable. L’équipe a aussi partagé des données détaillées sur l’effet de chaque composé, ce qui aidera d’autres chercheurs à développer des traitements sûrs et efficaces.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs vont maintenant travailler à transformer ces molécules protectrices en médicaments. Ils vont les tester davantage pour s’assurer qu’ils fonctionnent bien et sont sûrs pour les humains. Ce travail pourrait mener à de nouveaux traitements qui sauveront la vue de millions de personnes atteintes de maladies rétiniennes.

**Une phrase clé** – Des scientifiques ont identifié des substances chimiques et une enzyme clé qui protègent les cellules de l’œil responsables de la perception des couleurs et des détails, ouvrant la voie à de futurs traitements contre la perte de vision.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos descubren nueva forma de proteger las células clave para una visión clara y colorida
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los fotorreceptores cono son células especiales del ojo que nos ayudan a ver colores, leer y reconocer rostros. Estas células están en la mácula, la parte central de la retina. Cuando se dañan, como en enfermedades como la degeneración macular relacionada con la edad, las personas pierden la visión central. Hasta ahora, no existían tratamientos para detener este daño.

**Qué pasó** – Un equipo liderado por Botond Roska en el Instituto de Oftalmología Molecular y Clínica de Basilea probó más de 2,700 compuestos químicos en 20,000 modelos retinianos humanos cultivados en laboratorio. Buscaron sustancias que pudieran proteger las células cono de morir. Encontraron varias moléculas que ayudaron a mantener estas células saludables. También descubrieron que bloquear una enzima llamada caseína quinasa 1 puede proteger los conos.

**Impacto** – Este hallazgo es importante porque muestra una nueva forma de detener la pérdida de visión causada por daño a las células cono. El estudio usó modelos humanos y confirmó resultados en ratones, haciendo el descubrimiento más confiable. El equipo también compartió datos detallados sobre cómo cada compuesto afecta a las células cono, lo que ayudará a otros científicos a desarrollar tratamientos seguros y efectivos.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores ahora se enfocarán en convertir estas moléculas protectoras en medicamentos reales. Las probarán más para asegurarse de que funcionen bien y sean seguros para las personas. Este trabajo podría llevar a nuevos tratamientos que salven la vista de millones afectados por enfermedades retinianas.

**Una frase para recordar** – Los científicos han identificado químicos y una enzima clave que protegen las células del ojo que detectan color y detalles, abriendo la puerta a futuros tratamientos para la pérdida de visión.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a glowing human eye’s macula area depicted as a vibrant, colorful mosaic of cone-shaped crystals gently protected by delicate, translucent hands made of light, surrounded by softly illuminated molecular structures symbolizing protective compounds, all rendered in natural, soft earth tones and gentle blues.

articles.title: What’s hiding inside colon cancer could change treatment
articles.summary: Colorectal cancer may carry a unique microbial “fingerprint,” setting it apart from other cancers and opening a new frontier in diagnosis and treatment. By analyzing DNA from over 9,000 patients, researchers discovered that only colorectal tumors consistently host distinct microbial communities—challenging the long-held belief that all cancers have their own microbial signatures.
articles.content:
Colorectal cancer appears to stand apart from other cancers in a surprising way. New research from the University of East Anglia suggests it carries its own distinct microbial "fingerprint," a discovery that could reshape how doctors understand and treat the disease.

Colorectal cancer is the fourth most common cancer in the UK and the second leading cause of cancer-related death. The new findings may help clinicians better understand how the disease develops, how aggressive it may become, and how patients might respond to different treatments.

The team analyzed whole genome sequencing (WGS) data from more than 9,000 cancer patients. Their results, published in Science Translational Medicine, also challenge a widely held assumption that every cancer type has its own unique microbial signature.

"This study changes how we think about the role of microbes in cancer," said lead researcher Dr. Abraham Gihawi, from UEA's Norwich Medical School.

How scientists analyzed tumor microbes

To carry out the study, researchers examined Genomics England DNA sequence data from 11,735 cancer samples spanning 22 different cancer types.

"When you collect cancer DNA sequences, you also gain information from the DNA of microbes contained within the samples," said Dr. Gihawi.

"We wanted to determine the precise DNA composition of microbes present in each sample. So, we developed computer programs to remove human DNA and analyse the remaining microbe DNA.

"We then correlated this information with clinical data from the patients about their cancer type and clinical outcome.

"What we found challenges previous claims that each cancer type has a distinct microbiological signature or fingerprint.

"But importantly, as whole genome sequencing becomes more common in hospitals, we show that looking at the microbes in tumor samples could become a powerful tool for improving cancer care at little extra cost.

Colorectal cancer shows a distinct microbial signature

The analysis revealed a clear pattern. Among all cancers studied, only colorectal tumors consistently showed a unique and identifiable microbial community.

"Our results show that only colorectal tumors possess distinctly identifiable microbial communities.

"We found that these microbial signatures were so specific that they could accurately distinguish colorectal tumors from other tumors. We hope that this could help doctors diagnose the disease more precisely and researchers to study the microbes found in colorectal cancer."

The study also points to broader clinical uses. In oral cancers, researchers found that certain viruses such as HPV (human papillomavirus) could be detected more accurately than with some current diagnostic tests.

They also identified rare but dangerous viruses, including Human T-Lymphotropic Virus-1 (HTLV-1), which can remain dormant in the body and later contribute to cancer development.

Microbes linked to survival and treatment response

The findings suggest that microbes may do more than simply exist alongside cancer. In some cases, they appear to be linked to how patients fare.

"We found that certain types of bacteria were associated with poorer survival rates in some cases of sarcoma. This might lead to additional research and treatment options for these types of cancer," said Dr. Gihawi.

"One of the most exciting things we found was that in some sarcoma cases, the presence of specific bacteria was linked to better survival rates.

"This suggests that microbes might one day help doctors predict how well a patient will respond to treatment and open up new approaches to treatment," he added.

Whole genome sequencing as a clinical tool

Experts say the work highlights the growing importance of genome sequencing in modern medicine.

Prof Daniel Brewer, from UEA's Norwich Medical School, said: "This study highlights the growing clinical value of whole genome sequencing in identifying pathogenic organisms such as HTLV-1 and papillomavirus, which may otherwise go undetected.

"By revealing these hidden infections and providing insight into cancer prognosis -- particularly in sarcomas -- it demonstrates how genomic analysis is becoming an indispensable tool in precision medicine.

"The findings also suggest that oral cancer, in some cases, may be a close diagnostic consideration, further emphasizing the importance of comprehensive genomic profiling in clinical decision-making."

Collaboration and funding

The project was led by UEA and involved researchers from multiple institutions, including the University of Leeds, the Quadram Institute, Oxford Nanopore Technologies, the Institute of Cancer Research, London, the University of Manchester, National Institute for Health and Care Research (NIHR) Manchester Biomedical Research Centre, the University of Athens (Greece) the University of Liverpool, Cambridge University Hospitals NHS Foundation Trust, University College London, the University of Southampton, the University of North Carolina (US) and the Earlham Institute.

Funding for the study was provided by the Big C Cancer Charity and Prostate Cancer UK.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery that colorectal cancer uniquely carries a distinct microbial fingerprint, which challenges previous assumptions and opens new avenues for diagnosis and treatment. This breakthrough has broad implications for cancer care, potentially improving diagnosis accuracy, treatment response prediction, and patient outcomes on a large scale. The study is well-detailed, involving a large sample size and multiple institutions, highlighting its substance and significance.
articles.personality_title: Unique microbial fingerprint found in colorectal cancer may improve diagnosis and treatment
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Colorectal cancer is a common and deadly disease, ranking as the fourth most frequent cancer in the UK and the second leading cause of cancer deaths. Scientists have long believed that different cancers might each have their own unique microbes living inside tumors.

**What happened** – Researchers at the University of East Anglia studied DNA from over 9,000 cancer patients, looking closely at both human and microbial DNA in tumor samples. They discovered that only colorectal cancer tumors consistently contain a special set of microbes, creating a unique microbial “fingerprint” that sets them apart from other cancers.

**Impact** – This is important because it challenges the old idea that all cancers have their own microbial signatures. The unique microbes in colorectal tumors could help doctors diagnose the cancer more accurately and understand how aggressive it might be. The study also found that some microbes are linked to how well patients survive or respond to treatment, which could lead to better-personalized care.

**What's next step** – Scientists hope to use whole genome sequencing, a method that reads all the DNA in a sample, to detect these microbial fingerprints in hospitals. This could improve cancer diagnosis and help doctors decide the best treatments. Future research may explore how these microbes affect cancer growth and treatment success.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists have found that colorectal cancer tumors carry a unique group of microbes, offering new ways to diagnose and treat this disease more effectively.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une empreinte microbienne unique découverte dans le cancer colorectal pourrait améliorer le diagnostic et le traitement
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le cancer colorectal est un cancer courant et mortel, étant le quatrième cancer le plus fréquent au Royaume-Uni et la deuxième cause de décès par cancer. On pensait que chaque type de cancer pouvait avoir ses propres microbes spécifiques dans les tumeurs.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des chercheurs de l'Université d'East Anglia ont étudié l'ADN de plus de 9 000 patients atteints de cancer, en analysant à la fois l'ADN humain et microbien dans les échantillons de tumeurs. Ils ont découvert que seuls les cancers colorectaux contiennent systématiquement un ensemble particulier de microbes, formant une « empreinte » microbienne unique.

**Impact** – Cette découverte remet en question l'idée que tous les cancers possèdent une signature microbienne propre. Ces microbes uniques dans les tumeurs colorectales pourraient aider les médecins à diagnostiquer le cancer avec plus de précision et à mieux comprendre son agressivité. L'étude a aussi montré que certains microbes sont liés à la survie et à la réponse aux traitements des patients, ce qui pourrait améliorer les soins personnalisés.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques espèrent utiliser le séquençage complet du génome, une méthode qui lit tout l'ADN d'un échantillon, dans les hôpitaux pour détecter ces empreintes microbiennes. Cela pourrait améliorer le diagnostic du cancer et aider à choisir les traitements les plus adaptés. De futures recherches étudieront comment ces microbes influencent la croissance du cancer et le succès des traitements.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Les chercheurs ont découvert que les tumeurs du cancer colorectal possèdent un groupe unique de microbes, ouvrant la voie à de nouvelles méthodes pour mieux diagnostiquer et traiter cette maladie.
articles.personality_title_es: Descubren huella microbiana única en el cáncer colorrectal que puede mejorar diagnóstico y tratamiento
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El cáncer colorrectal es un cáncer común y mortal, siendo el cuarto más frecuente en el Reino Unido y la segunda causa principal de muertes por cáncer. Se creía que cada tipo de cáncer podría tener microbios propios en los tumores.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Universidad de East Anglia analizaron el ADN de más de 9,000 pacientes con cáncer, estudiando tanto el ADN humano como el microbiano en muestras de tumores. Descubrieron que solo los tumores colorrectales contienen consistentemente un grupo especial de microbios, formando una «huella» microbiana única.

**Impacto** – Esto es importante porque desafía la idea anterior de que todos los cánceres tienen su propia firma microbiana. Los microbios únicos en tumores colorrectales podrían ayudar a los médicos a diagnosticar el cáncer con mayor precisión y entender qué tan agresivo es. Además, algunos microbios están relacionados con la supervivencia y la respuesta al tratamiento, lo que podría mejorar la atención personalizada.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos esperan usar la secuenciación completa del genoma, una técnica que lee todo el ADN en una muestra, en hospitales para detectar estas huellas microbianas. Esto podría mejorar el diagnóstico y ayudar a elegir los mejores tratamientos. Futuras investigaciones explorarán cómo estos microbios afectan el crecimiento del cáncer y el éxito del tratamiento.

**Resumen en una frase** – Los científicos encontraron que los tumores de cáncer colorrectal tienen un grupo único de microbios, ofreciendo nuevas formas de diagnosticar y tratar esta enfermedad con mayor eficacia.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a glowing, stylized human colon shaped like a gently curved river, filled with diverse, colorful microscopic microbes resembling friendly, intricate patterns of bacteria and viruses, illuminated softly from within to symbolize discovery and hope, set against a simple, natural background of soft earth tones.

articles.title: New microwave frying technique could make french fries much healthier
articles.summary: Scientists have discovered a way to make French fries less greasy without ruining their taste. By combining regular frying with microwave heating, they reduce the amount of oil absorbed during cooking. The key lies in pressure inside the food—microwaves help push oil out instead of letting it seep in. The result: faster cooking, lower fat, and fries that can still stay crispy.
articles.content:
Fried foods are widely enjoyed, but their high fat content is linked to health issues such as obesity and hypertension. Creating lower fat versions that still deliver the same taste and texture could help consumers make healthier choices without feeling like they are missing out.

Researchers at the University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign are studying how microwave frying can improve the way French fries are made. Their findings suggest that combining traditional frying with microwave heating may reduce oil absorption while maintaining the crispy texture people expect. This approach could also shorten cooking times, making it appealing for large-scale food production.

"Consumers want healthy foods, but at the time of purchase, their cravings often take over. High oil content adds flavor, but it also contains a lot of energy and calories. My research team studies frying with the aim of obtaining lower fat content without significant differences in taste and texture," said principal investigator Pawan Singh Takhar, professor of food engineering in the Department of Food Science and Human Nutrition, part of the College of Agricultural, Consumer and Environmental Sciences at U of I.

Takhar and doctoral student Yash Shah outlined their findings in two recent studies focused on how microwave frying changes what happens inside French fries during cooking.

Inside the Frying Process

In one study, the team partnered with researchers at Washington State University to use a specially designed microwave fryer. This system operated at two frequencies, 2.45 gigahertz (similar to a regular microwave oven) and 5.8 gigahertz.

To prepare the samples, potatoes were rinsed, peeled, cut into strips, blanched, and salted. The strips were then fried in soybean oil heated to 180 degrees Celsius. During and after frying, the team measured temperature, pressure, volume, texture, moisture, and oil content.

A key challenge in frying is preventing oil from entering the food, Takhar explained. Early in the process, the potato's pores are filled with water, leaving no space for oil. As cooking continues, that water evaporates, creating empty spaces that allow oil to be drawn in through negative pressure.

"Think about a straw in a drink. If you push air into the straw, it creates positive pressure and any liquid will be pushed out. But if you suck on the straw, the liquid moves upward. Now imagine food materials have lots of tiny straws. When there is positive pressure, the oil stays out. But if there is negative pressure, the oil starts moving in," Takhar explained.

How Microwaves Help Reduce Oil Absorption

Much of the frying process occurs under negative pressure, which increases the tendency for oil to be pulled into the food. The researchers aimed to extend the time under positive pressure and reduce the period when negative pressure dominates.

"When we heat something in a conventional oven, the heat moves from outside to inside, but a microwave oven heats from the inside out, because the microwaves penetrate everywhere in the material. The microwaves oscillate water molecules, causing more vapor formation and thus shifting the pressure profile towards the positive side. The higher pressure in microwaves helps reduce oil penetration," Takhar said.

Because microwaves generate heat throughout the food, they promote vapor formation and help maintain internal pressure that keeps oil from being absorbed as easily.

Faster Cooking and Less Oil

Alongside the experimental work, the researchers developed mathematical models to better understand how different factors influence frying. This modeling allowed them to examine temperature, pressure, moisture, texture, volume, and oil content under different conditions, including 2.45 GHz, 5.8 GHz, and conventional frying.

The results showed that microwave frying led to quicker moisture loss, reduced cooking times, and lower oil uptake overall.

However, microwave frying alone does not produce the desired texture.

"However, if you just use microwave frying, you get soggy food. To obtain a crispy texture and taste, you need conventional heating. Therefore, we propose combining the two approaches in the same unit. Conventional heating maintains the crispiness, while microwave heating lowers the oil intake," Takhar said.

A Practical Solution for the Food Industry

The researchers suggest that existing industrial fryers could be upgraded with microwave generators, which are relatively low cost and widely available. This makes the combined method a practical option for large-scale food production.

The findings were published in two papers. The first, "The Effect of Conventional and Microwave Frying on the Quality Characteristics of French Fries," appeared in the Journal of Food Science and was authored by Yash Shah, Xu Zhou, Juming Tang, and Pawan Singh Takhar.

The second paper "Predicting the quality changes during microwave frying of food biopolymers by solving the hybrid mixture theory-based unsaturated transport, and electromagnetics equations," was published in Current Research in Food Science.

The research was funded by USDA National Institute of Food and Agriculture (Awards 2020-67017-31194, ILLU-698-308, and ILLU-698-926).
articles.reasoning: The article reports a scientific breakthrough in food preparation that significantly reduces oil absorption in French fries, addressing health concerns related to high fat intake. The method combines microwave and conventional frying to maintain taste and texture while lowering fat content, which can benefit a broad population by promoting healthier eating habits. The research is detailed, practical for industrial application, and supported by credible institutions, making the impact meaningful and well-substantiated.
articles.personality_title: New microwave frying method makes French fries healthier and crispier
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – French fries are a popular food but usually contain a lot of oil, which can lead to health problems like obesity and high blood pressure. Scientists want to find ways to make fries with less fat but still tasty and crispy.

**What happened** – Researchers at the University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign discovered a way to cook French fries by combining regular frying with microwave heating. This method uses microwaves to create pressure inside the fries that pushes oil out instead of letting it soak in. They tested fries cooked with this new method and found they absorbed less oil, cooked faster, and stayed crispy.

**Impact** – This method is important because it lowers the fat in French fries without changing their taste or texture. The microwaves heat the fries from the inside, creating steam that keeps oil from entering. This means people can enjoy fries that are less greasy but still delicious. Also, the cooking time is shorter, which could help food factories make fries more efficiently.

**What's next step** – The researchers suggest that factories could add microwave devices to their existing fryers to use this new method. They hope this will lead to healthier fried foods being made on a large scale. Further studies may also explore how this technique can be used for other fried foods.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Combining microwave heating with traditional frying reduces oil in French fries while keeping them crispy and tasty, offering a healthier way to enjoy this favorite snack.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une nouvelle méthode de friture au micro-ondes rend les frites plus saines et croustillantes
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les frites sont un plat très apprécié mais contiennent souvent beaucoup d'huile, ce qui peut causer des problèmes de santé comme l'obésité et l'hypertension. Les scientifiques cherchent des moyens de réduire la graisse tout en gardant le goût et le croustillant.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des chercheurs de l'Université de l'Illinois ont trouvé une façon de cuire les frites en combinant la friture traditionnelle et le chauffage au micro-ondes. Cette méthode utilise les micro-ondes pour créer une pression à l'intérieur des frites qui chasse l'huile au lieu de la laisser pénétrer. Ils ont testé cette technique et constaté que les frites absorbent moins d'huile, cuisent plus vite et restent croustillantes.

**Impact** – Cette méthode est importante car elle diminue la quantité de graisse sans changer le goût ni la texture des frites. Les micro-ondes chauffent les frites de l'intérieur, générant de la vapeur qui empêche l'huile de pénétrer. Ainsi, les consommateurs peuvent manger des frites moins grasses mais toujours savoureuses. De plus, le temps de cuisson est réduit, ce qui peut aider les usines à produire plus efficacement.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs suggèrent que les usines pourraient ajouter des générateurs de micro-ondes à leurs friteuses existantes pour utiliser cette méthode. Ils espèrent que cela permettra de produire à grande échelle des aliments frits plus sains. D'autres études pourraient aussi étudier l'application de cette technique à d'autres aliments frits.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Combiner chauffage au micro-ondes et friture traditionnelle réduit l'huile dans les frites tout en gardant leur croustillant et leur goût, offrant une façon plus saine de savourer ce snack préféré.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevo método de fritura con microondas hace las papas fritas más saludables y crujientes
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Las papas fritas son un alimento muy popular, pero suelen contener mucho aceite, lo que puede causar problemas de salud como obesidad y presión alta. Los científicos buscan formas de hacer papas fritas con menos grasa pero que sigan siendo sabrosas y crujientes.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Universidad de Illinois descubrieron una forma de cocinar papas fritas combinando la fritura tradicional con calentamiento por microondas. Este método usa microondas para crear presión dentro de las papas que empuja el aceite hacia afuera en vez de dejar que se absorba. Probando esta técnica, encontraron que las papas absorben menos aceite, se cocinan más rápido y permanecen crujientes.

**Impacto** – Este método es importante porque reduce la grasa en las papas fritas sin cambiar su sabor ni textura. Las microondas calientan las papas desde dentro, creando vapor que evita que el aceite penetre. Esto significa que la gente puede disfrutar papas menos grasosas pero igual de ricas. Además, el tiempo de cocción es menor, lo que podría ayudar a fábricas a producir papas más rápido.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores sugieren que las fábricas podrían agregar generadores de microondas a sus freidoras actuales para usar este método. Esperan que esto permita producir alimentos fritos más saludables a gran escala. También podrían estudiar cómo aplicar esta técnica a otros alimentos fritos.

**Frase clave** – Combinar el calentamiento por microondas con la fritura tradicional reduce el aceite en las papas fritas manteniéndolas crujientes y sabrosas, ofreciendo una forma más saludable de disfrutar este snack favorito.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of golden French fries partially immersed in a stylized oil bath that gently swirls away from the fries, while soft waves of microwave energy—depicted as translucent, warm-colored ripples—penetrate the fries from within, highlighting a balance between crispiness and lightness, all rendered in natural, simple tones without any human figures.

articles.title: I have been bitten by more than 200 snakes – on purpose
articles.summary: If you are unlucky enough to have been bitten by a snake, you are unlikely to want to repeat the experience. Not so for Tim Friede, who intentionally exposes himself to deadly bites in the hope of developing a treatment for the 5 million people who are bitten each year
articles.content:
If you are unlucky enough to have been bitten by a snake, you are unlikely to want to repeat the experience. Not so for Tim Friede, who intentionally exposes himself to deadly bites in the hope of developing a treatment for the 5 million people who are bitten each year

Tim Friede with a water cobra Centivax

I know what it feels like to almost die from a snake bite. You can’t move. You can’t breathe. Your diaphragm’s frozen. But you can hear everything. So when I was in the ICU, I could hear the doctors talking about me. “Why did he do it? Was it a suicide attempt?” And I’m like: no, it wasn’t. I just screwed up.

I began injecting myself with snake venom in 2001, in an effort to develop a treatment. If you look at the numbers, 5 million people are bitten every year. There are 138,000 deaths every year and 400,000-plus amputations and other complications. Those are pretty big numbers.

There are organisations that want to help, like the Strike Out Snakebite global initiative, which is looking to raise awareness of the impact of snake-bite envenoming. And there is snake antivenom, first invented 125 years ago by Albert Calmette. But these haven’t changed much in that time, and they aren’t perfect. They’re made by injecting horses with venom, then collecting the antibodies the horses make. Using these in humans, you run the risk of anaphylactic shock because the antivenom is based off of a foreign equine protein.

I wanted to take the horse out of the picture, but I didn’t want to die. I didn’t want to lose a hand. I didn’t even want to miss work.

I had already taken a small course on extracting venom from spiders, scorpions and centipedes. That was back in 1999. So it wasn’t too difficult to extract snake venom. I began with cobra venom, injecting it at a dilution of 1 in 10,000. You don’t feel those doses too much. It’s kind of like a small bee sting. But slowly through time, I built up the concentration to lethal doses – to pure venom.

Free newsletter Sign up to Eight Weeks to a Healthier You Your science-backed guide to the easy habits that will help you sleep well, stress less, eat smarter and age better. Sign up to newsletter

Then I was ready for live snake bites. It was nerve-wracking because I didn’t know for sure how immune I was. I didn’t know if I was going to make it or not. There’s no book on it. There’s no school that can teach you how to do it. I had to teach myself and just use my own body for the experiment.

And it was a horrible start. It was Wednesday 12 September 2001, at 11:02pm. I remember it clearly: I took a cobra bite, then waited an hour and took another cobra bite. The first one, I was fine with. But for the second one, I had no immunity because all my antibodies were bound to the venom from the first bite. At 12:00am, I dropped. Flatlined. I was in the ICU in a coma for four days. They had to give me antivenom from the local zoo. I actually had antivenom back at my house, but the ambulance crew didn’t know that.

When I got out of the hospital, I realised: I could either quit because I screwed up or I could learn from the experience and carry on. I did the latter. I have had a little over 200 bites now and I have never used antivenom again.

I got really serious about it. I started reaching out to scientists. There is a long history of self-experimentation in medicine. I have a signed personal letter from Barry Marshall, who self-medicated and won a Nobel prize. I also spoke to Peter Doherty, another Nobel winner for immunity. And I’m like: “Holy crap, these people actually take me seriously.” So that’s when I got really big into academia, studying venom.

Snake venom, even if it’s the same genus and species, can be completely different. The perfect example is the brown snake, Pseudonaja textilis, in Australia. In Queensland, to the north, its venom is different than it is in the south. And that’s the problem with antivenoms: they make it for a certain area, so it might not work in another area.

I wanted to develop broad-spectrum antibodies in my blood that could protect against all venoms. So I kept working with different snakes from all over the world. There are around 650 species of venomous snakes and I knew I wasn’t going to inject venom from all of them, because I can’t access all of them. I just tried to pick the most dangerous ones I could get: taipans – they’re the most venomous snakes in the world – cobras, kraits, coral snakes, rattlesnakes.

Taipan bites are actually pretty easy. It’s pretty much a pure neurotoxin. But vipers and pit-vipers, their venom contains necrotic components that destroy muscle. That’s completely different. The pain is huge.

I have been studied six times over 25 years now. That was the most important thing for me. I wanted to be studied because if I’m not studied, I can’t contribute to new antivenoms. The most recent is the most important. Jacob Glanville at the vaccine company Centivax contacted me because of a YouTube video I did, with a black mamba bite and a taipan bite back to back. I shipped him blood and they extracted DNA from my B-cells to clone my IgG antibodies. Then we started doing in-vivo studies with mice.

The studies were so intense, but the lab is so good. I found out – and this is pretty trippy – that we can neutralise the venom of the king cobra (Ophiophagus hannah) with my antibodies, and I’ve never used king cobra in my experiments. So that gave us hope that we can reach a broad-spectrum level, a universal antivenom.

Last year, the paper came out in Cell Press. It took almost 25 years to get to that paper. My name isn’t on the author list. I used myself as a test subject and some people in academia look down on that. We got kicked out of a lot of journals, denied publication when my name was on the author list. But I have no problem with that. I don’t want accolades.

It will still take a long, long time to get from the mice to an antivenom we can use in humans, but I have no problem waiting. I’ll wait till the day I die. But I can sleep really good at night knowing that I did everything I possibly could to make a difference.

As told to Colin Barras
articles.reasoning: The article describes a real and significant effort by Tim Friede to develop a broad-spectrum antivenom through self-experimentation, addressing a major global health issue affecting millions annually. The story has broad significance due to the large number of snakebite victims worldwide and the limitations of current antivenoms. It provides substantial context about the challenges and scientific progress, including collaboration with researchers and promising lab results, making it an inspiring and hopeful medical breakthrough.
articles.personality_title: Man survives 200 snake bites to help create better antivenom
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context**
Each year, around 5 million people worldwide suffer snake bites. Current treatments use antivenoms made from horse antibodies, which can cause serious allergic reactions and don’t always work well for all snake species or regions.

**What happened**
Tim Friede started injecting himself with snake venom in 2001 to build immunity and help develop a new kind of antivenom. Despite a near-fatal cobra bite in 2001 that put him in a coma for four days, he continued his work. Over 25 years, he has been bitten more than 200 times by some of the world’s deadliest snakes, including cobras, taipans, and rattlesnakes. He worked with scientists to study his antibodies and found they can neutralize venom from snakes he never even tested on himself.

**Impact**
Friede’s unique approach could lead to a broad-spectrum antivenom that protects against many snake venoms worldwide. This is important because snake venom varies by species and location, and current antivenoms are limited to specific snakes and regions. His antibodies even neutralized king cobra venom, which he had not injected, showing promise for a universal treatment.

**What’s next step**
Scientists are now testing Friede’s antibodies in mice to develop a new antivenom for humans. It will take time—possibly many years—before a safe and effective product is available. Friede remains patient, hoping his work will eventually save many lives.

**One-sentence takeaway**
By risking his own life to build immunity, Tim Friede is helping create a new antivenom that could protect people from many different snake bites worldwide.
articles.personality_title_fr: Un homme survit à plus de 200 morsures de serpent pour créer un meilleur antivenin
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte**
Chaque année, environ 5 millions de personnes dans le monde sont mordues par des serpents. Les traitements actuels utilisent des antivenins fabriqués à partir d’anticorps de cheval, qui peuvent provoquer de graves réactions allergiques et ne fonctionnent pas toujours bien pour toutes les espèces ou régions.

**Ce qui s’est passé**
Tim Friede a commencé à s’injecter du venin de serpent en 2001 pour développer son immunité et aider à créer un nouvel antivenin. Malgré une morsure de cobra presque fatale qui l’a plongé dans le coma pendant quatre jours, il a continué. En 25 ans, il a été mordu plus de 200 fois par certains des serpents les plus dangereux du monde, comme les cobras, les taipans et les crotales. Il a collaboré avec des scientifiques qui ont découvert que ses anticorps peuvent neutraliser le venin de serpents qu’il n’a jamais testés sur lui.

**Impact**
L’approche unique de Friede pourrait mener à un antivenin à large spectre protégeant contre de nombreux venins de serpents dans le monde. C’est important car les venins varient selon l’espèce et la région, et les antivenins actuels sont limités à certains serpents et lieux. Ses anticorps neutralisent même le venin du cobra royal, qu’il n’a jamais injecté, ce qui montre le potentiel d’un traitement universel.

**Prochaines étapes**
Les scientifiques testent maintenant les anticorps de Friede sur des souris pour développer un nouvel antivenin pour les humains. Cela prendra du temps—peut-être plusieurs années—avant qu’un produit sûr et efficace soit disponible. Friede reste patient, espérant que son travail sauvera de nombreuses vies.

**Une phrase clé**
En risquant sa vie pour développer son immunité, Tim Friede aide à créer un nouvel antivenin capable de protéger contre de nombreuses morsures de serpent dans le monde.
articles.personality_title_es: Hombre sobrevive a más de 200 mordeduras de serpiente para crear un mejor antiveneno
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto**
Cada año, alrededor de 5 millones de personas en el mundo son mordidas por serpientes. Los tratamientos actuales usan antivenenos hechos con anticuerpos de caballos, que pueden causar reacciones alérgicas graves y no siempre funcionan bien para todas las especies o regiones.

**Qué pasó**
Tim Friede comenzó a inyectarse veneno de serpiente en 2001 para crear inmunidad y ayudar a desarrollar un nuevo antiveneno. A pesar de una mordedura de cobra casi fatal que lo dejó en coma por cuatro días, continuó con su trabajo. En 25 años, ha sido mordido más de 200 veces por algunas de las serpientes más peligrosas del mundo, como cobras, taipanes y serpientes de cascabel. Trabajó con científicos que descubrieron que sus anticuerpos pueden neutralizar veneno de serpientes que él nunca probó en sí mismo.

**Impacto**
El enfoque único de Friede podría crear un antiveneno de amplio espectro que proteja contra muchos venenos de serpientes en todo el mundo. Esto es importante porque el veneno varía según la especie y la región, y los antivenenos actuales son limitados a ciertas serpientes y lugares. Sus anticuerpos incluso neutralizaron el veneno de la cobra real, que él nunca se había inyectado, mostrando esperanza para un tratamiento universal.

**Próximos pasos**
Los científicos están probando ahora los anticuerpos de Friede en ratones para desarrollar un nuevo antiveneno para humanos. Esto tomará tiempo—posiblemente muchos años—antes de que haya un producto seguro y efectivo disponible. Friede tiene paciencia y espera que su trabajo salve muchas vidas.

**Una frase clave**
Al arriesgar su vida para crear inmunidad, Tim Friede ayuda a desarrollar un antiveneno nuevo que podría proteger contra muchas mordeduras de serpiente en todo el mundo.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a calm human silhouette surrounded by gently coiled venomous snakes—cobras, taipans, and black mambas—whose fangs glow softly but do not harm, symbolizing controlled exposure and resilience, with delicate antibody-like strands weaving through the scene representing immunity and healing.
